title: Add ability to evict a container
body:
Add ability to evict a container when it becomes unusable. This may
happen when the host setup changes after a container creation, making it
impossible for that container to be used or removed.

--- 

This may happen when you create a container with a specific runtime in libpod.conf, and later the runtime name is changed in the the conf, with outcome:
```bash
$ sudo podman run -d --runtime foo-runtime alpine sleep 12345
add950497c58b4004aee3a6077658615882f8487f9dca4a337fad14c01fb4077

$ sudo sed -i "s/foo-runtime/bar-runtime/" /etc/containers/libpod.conf

$ sudo podman ps -a
ERRO[0000] Error retrieving container add950497c58b4004aee3a6077658615882f8487f9dca4a337fad14c01fb4077 from the database: cannot find OCI runtime "foo-runtime" for container add950497c58b4004aee3a6077658615882f8487f9dca4a337fad14c01fb4077: runtime not available in the current configuration
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                        COMMAND               CREATED      STATUS                     PORTS                                          NAMES
50c843e037fe  localhost/cups-epson:latest  cupsd -f              2 hours ago  Exited (0) 31 minutes ago  0.0.0.0:6631->631/tcp, 0.0.0.0:3000->3000/tcp  suspicious_germain
c684f0d469f2  k8s.gcr.io/pause:3.1                               9 days ago   Exited (0) 31 minutes ago  0.0.0.0:6631->631/tcp, 0.0.0.0:3000->3000/tcp  e812f6be094a-infra

$ sudo podman rm -ff ad
add950497c58b4004aee3a6077658615882f8487f9dca4a337fad14c01fb4077


```

@marcov, CI isn't happy yet:
```
[+0001s] tree is dirty, please remove API.md, then "make varlink_api_generate" and commit changes.
[+0001s] 
[+0001s]  M API.md
```
@marcov, could you squash the commits? In case some don't pass tests, it can make bisecting errors harder.

> Just a few nits and the request to not change the existing varlink API. @marcov, can you elaborate a bit why we need new level of removal? I didn't follow previous discussions and will likely miss this information. I feel like just removing the containers with an invalid config by default (i.e., just extend the semantics of removal).

Hi @vrothberg. 
Problem: containers as stored in the state DB with their config, containing among other things information about the OCI runtime name and paths. If the OCI runtime name changes in libpod.conf, of if the OCI runtime path does not point to the original runtime, then you can't do much with this container, apart from removing it.
What the evict API does is:
- retrieve from the state a container ID (instead of a `*Container`).
- remove from the state and from the storage that container ID with minimal checks.

Compared to the remove API, the evict does not try to terminate the processes associated with the container, since it's not possible to interact with the OCI runtime.

@vrothberg thanks for the review. I squashed and applied the updates. 

> > Compared to the remove API, the evict does not try to terminate the processes associated with the container, since it's not possible to interact with the OCI runtime.
> 
> That's quite a difference. For the Varlink API, I suggest adding a `EvictContainer(...)` function. 


So you're suggesting to:
1. add in `pkg/varlinkapi/containers.go` a `func (i *LibpodAPI) EvictContainer(...)` 
2. have that called from `pkg/adapter/containers_remote.go:RemoveContainers(...)` based on the passed flag?

> On the CLI, I prefer an `podman rm --evict` flag, rather than mixing that with force - especially since containers are not stopped.

Got it, the `-ff` was suggested a @mheon, I am fine both ways. Using `-ff` has the advantage of having to remember one less flag :-)

> So you're suggesting to:
> 
>     1. add in `pkg/varlinkapi/containers.go` a `func (i *LibpodAPI) EvictContainer(...)`
> 
>     2. have that called from `pkg/adapter/containers_remote.go:RemoveContainers(...)` based on the passed flag?
> 
> 
> > On the CLI, I prefer an `podman rm --evict` flag, rather than mixing that with force - especially since containers are not stopped.
> 
> Got it, the `-ff` was suggested a @mheon, I am fine both ways. Using `-ff` has the advantage of having to remember one less flag :-)

I would keep `evict` and `remove` separate on both, the API level and the CLI. The semantics seem too different to mix them together. My most important concern is to not break the existing API and I will leave the decision regarding `-ff` vs. `--evict` to @mheon.
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably #3143) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.
Rebased to resolve conflicts and added varlink API EvictContainer.
Going to give this another look this afternoon or tomorrow - sorry for delays, been very busy over here
Overall direction looks solid. Would be nice to have some unit tests for the new State functions in state_test.go.

sorry for being late at the review.

Is the new flag useful only in the case a runtime is not available anymore or are there other cases?

I'd expect `-f` to behave like that by default.  It should try to cleanup as much as possible without the need of another flag.

> Is the new flag useful only in the case a runtime is not available anymore or are there other cases?

@giuseppe as of now eviction would only be used if the container runtime is unavailable or has changed, while the `-f` flag is, according to the help, used to force stopping a container before remove.
@vrothberg, @jwhonce, @mheon: I addressed your latest comments with the last push.
@marcov, I'll take a look now. The CI errors seem legit as the linter isn't happy. You can run `make validate` locally to check.

> > 
> 
> @giuseppe as of now eviction would only be used if the container runtime is unavailable or has changed, while the `-f` flag is, according to the help, used to force stopping a container before remove.

can't we change the documentation and include this case as well?  It still seems like a very specific corner case to me, that can be handled with `-f` instead of another flag.

I don't think this new evict functionality should fall under --force; it's definitely a step beyond.

Whether we want `-ff` or `--evict` is still up for debate, but it seems like `--evict` is leading?

I would through my vote in for --force or --force --force.  I still see this as something that will just aggravate the user.  IE he tells podman to remove the container and it comes back and says,  I can't because it is in a weird state.  Then the user does a --force --force, and it removes it.  I as a user would react with WTF?

> I would through my vote in for --force or --force --force. I still see this as something that will just aggravate the user. IE he tells podman to remove the container and it comes back and says, I can't because it is in a weird state. Then the user does a --force --force, and it removes it. I as a user would react with WTF?

And what if I changed the runtime to `crun` and want to only clean up all old containers that ran with `runc`? The thing is that running `-ff` shows an inherently different behaviour, as it only removes containers with an invalid runtime and leaves the others untouched - no stopping etc. So it's not a functional or semantic extension to `-f`, in fact the sets of removed/evicted containers have zero overlap.

I wouldn't know how to explain the behaviour of `-f` vs `-ff` without starting the sentence with: "I know it sounds confusing ..." :^) All my confusion would disappear with a separate flag.

@vrothberg Not sure --evict does a better job of explaining this either.

Is the use case?

As a user I am switching from runc to crun, I have 2 containers which were created with runc and 3 created with crun.  I want to remove the runc containers, and I get an error.  

podman rm RUNCCID1 RUNCCID2
Error: Runtime no longer exists

Now I say:
podman rm --force RUNCCID1 RUNCCID2
Error: Runtime no longer exists

So now I have to find and try:

podman rm --evict RUNCCID1 RUNCCID2
Success

@rhatdan, yes that's one use case. I think that `--force` should also `--evict` containers as invalid configs shouldn't be special cased. However, `--evict` is still a nice way of cleaning up those containers (and only those).
FWIW, I concur with @vrothberg's last note.  `--force` should do current functionality and also `--evict`.  It's our big sledge hammer.  `--evict` would handle just those particular containers.

@vrothberg @TomSweeneyRedHat  note however that as of now `podman -a --evict` would not work, as this would require a duplicate of `GetContainersByContext` returning a set of IDs instead of Containers.

I've tried these steps with Docker:

- add a runtime to docker,
- create a container `$CTR_ID`
- remove the runtime.
- `docker rm -f $CTR_ID` removes the container without complaining

Similarly, `--force` for `rm` has a similar meaning of attempting deleting as much as possible without bothering the user.

If we want to treat "containers without a working runtime" differently, I think we can add a new filter to `ps` so the user can do something like `podman ps -q --filter runtime-status=not-existing | xargs podman rm -f`
I tend  to agree with @giuseppe, the *force* word can refer both to running and not usable containers, and it's just easier for the users to use `--force` in both situations.
Pushed unit tests for the new functions in `state.go`
Put me in the camp for just using --force.
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably #3766) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.

> So, me, @giuseppe and @rhatdan likes `--force`
> @vrothberg and @TomSweeneyRedHat wants` --evict`
> @mheon is for `--force --force`
> 
> Can I go and update the code with the most voted option?

I think there's a misunderstanding. In https://github.com/containers/libpod/pull/3549#issuecomment-517660899 I actually meant to go with `--force` only :) However, if we want to have a dedicated `--evict` then `--force` should imply doing `--evict`.

I believe that @TomSweeneyRedHat is on board with that as well.
Thanks @vrothberg!

Just to be sure we're clear.  I'm good with the `--evict` option which would get rid of the unusable containers.  When the `--force` is used it should do the evict functionality too, regardless of the `--evict` option being present.

I have the impression that the added value of introducing an extra `--evict` flag is limited, `--force` can remove unusable containers, and I don't see any apparent disadvantage of using the flag for both use cases.
pushed a temp commit to use `--force` flag for eviction too. @TomSweeneyRedHat I'd appreciate your review for the text changes.

@marcov The doc part looks fine and I'm fine moving forward with this as is if you can get the uhappy tests to complete.

Personally, I'd like to have the --evict flag still to remove just unusable images.  Otherwise if the user wants to remove just the unusable ones, they'd have to do `podman rm -f id1 id2 id3 ...`  rather then `podman rm --evict`.  But that's probably a small use case.

An incredibly small use case.  Not worth worrying about.
Lets stick with --force and get this merged.
We should also remove all changes to the varlink API etc. since we go with `--force`.

@vrothberg: if I remove the varlink API, I end up not being able to call the `EvictContainer` method from ` pkg/adapter/containers_remote.go` : `RemoveContainers`. Is there a trick to have to solve this?

> @vrothberg: if I remove the varlink API, I end up not being able to call the `EvictContainer` method from ` pkg/adapter/containers_remote.go` : `RemoveContainers`. Is there a trick to have to solve this?

Since we want `rm --force` to also evict a container, I assumed we don't need to expose `EvictContainer` anymore. Do we still need that?

I tried to remove that from `cmd/podman/varlink/io.podman.varlink` and `b/pkg/varlinkapi/containers.go`, but then `podman-remote` fails to build because of a call to `iopodman.EvictContainer` in `pkg/adapter/containers_remote.go : RemoveContainers`. I'm not sure how to handle that.

> I tried to remove that from `cmd/podman/varlink/io.podman.varlink` and `b/pkg/varlinkapi/containers.go`, but then `podman-remote` fails to build because of a call to `iopodman.EvictContainer` in `pkg/adapter/containers_remote.go : RemoveContainers`. I'm not sure how to handle that.

We need to revert the changes to `cmd/podman/varlink/io.podman.varlink` and the references to it in the code to get rid of the compilation error.

@vrothberg, if I do like you said, then how can I call `iopodman.EvictContainer` here?

https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/128a1707f7d9e8df648a6ba353bfa16b9bef2431/pkg/adapter/containers_remote.go#L323-L339

Why are we trying to force the Varlink API to follow CLI conventions? These
are separate operations and the API should treat them as such, even if we
decide to conflate in the command line.
